content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
-
June 17,
1993
Charles Kim
Steel Law Building
610 S. Harvard Blvd., Ste. ill
Los Angeles,
CA
90005
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—93—204
Dear Mr.
Kim:
You have requested advice concerning the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
!
QUESTION
Will funds received in connection with a retirement dinner
for Howard Kwon be considered contributions or gifts to Mr.
Kwon?
CONCLUSION
Because invitations to the dinner specifically advocate and
request support for Mr.
Kwon’s election to the Board af the ABC
Unified School District, the event appears to be more in the
nature of a political fundraiser.
Therefore, persons who attend
the dinner are making contributions to Mr.
Kwon.
FACTS
Howard Kwon is retiring from--teaching in the Los Angeles and
ABC Unified School Districts.
Mr.
Kwon has unofficially declared
his intention to run for the Board of the ABC Unified School
District.
A group of Mr.
Kwon’s friends have organized a
retirement dinner, with tickets selling for $50 per person, and
are hoping to raise $1,500 above the cost of the event as a gift
.to Mr.
Kwon.
Attendees are requested to make checks for the event
payable to Mr.
Kwon.
1’
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804.0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Charles Kim
Our File No.
A—93—204
Page 2
Some of the invitations to the event included an insert in
Korean which stated:
We have prepared a retirement dinner to honor Mr.
Howard Kwon who is retiring from more than 15 years
of teaching in Los Angeles and ABC Unified School
Districts.
We also would like to congratulate his
decision to run for ABC Unified School Board.
We
need more dedicated community leaders to participate
in the main stream’s decision making process,
and we
feel that Mr.
Kwon would make great contribution as
a school board member.
...Mr.
Howard Kwon made so much contribution for the
community,
now is the time for us to help Mr.
Kwon.
Once again we strongly urge you and your family to
attend this meaningful event.
(Emphasis added.)
ANALYSIS
• The Act requires candidates for state and local elective
offices to file periodic reports disclosing contributions received
and expenditures made in connection with their elections.
(Section 84100,
The term “candidate” is defined in
Section 82007 to mean:
...an individual who is lIsted on the ballot or
who has qualified to have write-in votes on his or
her behalf counted by election officials, for
nomination for or election to any elective office,
or who receives a contribution or makes an expenditure
or gives his or her consent for any other person to
receive a contribution or make—an expenditure with
a view to bringing about his or her nomination or
election to any elective office, . . .whether or not
he or she has announced his or her candidacy or
filed a declaration of candidacy at such time....
(Emphasis added.)
A “contribution” is any monetary or nonmonetary payment made
for political purposes for which full and adequate consideration
is not made to the donor.
A payment is made for political
purposes if it is ràéived by or made at the behest of a
candidate, unless it is clear from surrounding circumstances that
the payment was received for personal purposes unrelated to his or
her candidacy.
(Section 82015; Regulation 18215.)
Although your letter states that Mr.
Kwon’s candidacy was
mentioned in the invitation in error and that you did not intend


Charles Kim
Our File No.
A-93—204
Page 3
to conduct the dinner as a political fundraiser, we cannot avoid
the fact that the invitations clearly advocate and seek support
for Mr.
Kwon’ s candidacy.
Therefore,
-. we must conclude that the
event is more political than personal in nature and that payments
received in connection with the event are contributions to Mr.
Kwon.
Enclosed are the appropriate forms and informational
materials Mr.
Kwon will need in order to comply with the campaign
provisions of the Act.
If you have additional questions, please
contact me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
By:
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division
Enclosures

for Howard Kwon be considered contributions or gifts to Mr.

CONCLUSION: Because invitations to the dinner specifically advocate and
